"She looked like she knew where she was going," he said finally. "Not in a dramatic sense. She walked with the same pace she always walks. She did not look back at the chamber door. She went to the east corridor and she left." He paused. "She had her briefcase. The same one she has carried to every session since I've been security director. Brown, worn at the left handle." He paused again. "She looked finished."

"Finished," Corin said.

"Not as a judgment. As a description. Like something she had been carrying was now fully carried, and she had set it down. Not dropped it — set it down. Deliberately." Hollan stood. "That's the best I can give you."

"That's accurate," Corin said. "Thank you."

After Hollan left, Corin sat for a long time in his office.

The investigation was open. Derel's documentation was the primary source. The analysis Cross had compiled was in the evidentiary file. Elara Venn's motion was in the Council record. The deliberation record included Parren's address, and her vote, and the unanimous result.

Parren was gone.

The investigation would run without her. The Council would work through whatever it found without her. The Substrate Assessment Unit would be reviewed without her. She had voted to open the process and had left before it concluded.

He thought about what Hollan had said. Finished. Something she had been carrying was now fully carried, and she had set it down.

He thought about what it would take to set something down. After the weight of it. After the years.

He wrote the liaison log entry: *Council member Parren's term concluded. Final session: routine business, three items. Record signed. No farewell address filed. Session closed. Cross-reference: censure entry, motion 012-02, investigation authorization.*

He sent it to the administrative filing system.

Outside the window, the afternoon was moving toward evening. The investigation was open. The record existed. The next session would have a new member in Parren's seat, someone who would bring their own questions and their own gaps, and the institution would continue to be the institution that it had been, and also, in some small and fully documented way, different than it had been.

Corin closed his terminal and stayed at his desk in the quiet.

---

Two days after Parren's final session, Corin contacted Derel.

He contacted her because the investigation had opened and she was now a formal cooperating subject and he wanted to make sure she knew what that meant practically — not legally, she had legal guidance through the committee — but practically, in terms of what the next weeks would look like for the unit.

She answered promptly. She said: "I know what it means. The committee contacted me yesterday. They want to begin working through the data series archive — the full Series 7, not just the entries I previously provided. That will take time. I've made the archive available."

"How large is Series 7?"

"Forty thousand entries, approximately. The committee is starting with a systematic search using the coordinate's location data as the primary filter. That should narrow the relevant entries significantly."

He thought about forty thousand entries. He thought about the coordinate anomaly sitting in there — the signal change that had been filed under ANN-LPR fourteen months before the expedition, in a series with forty thousand other entries, not hidden, not flagged, just filed.

"The investigation's scope," he said. "The committee will be looking at the administrative history — not just the Exchange relationship but the classification systems, the review protocols. Is there documentation in the unit's files about the founding-era protocols? The original classification code specifications?"

"Yes," Derel said. "The founding documentation is in a separate archive from the operational records. I've been holding it in reserve — no one asked for it specifically, and I didn't know if it was within the investigation's scope. If the committee wants it, it's available."

"They'll want it," Corin said.

"I'll flag it for the committee contact today."

He was quiet for a moment. "Derel. The investigation is going to be longer than the initial scope suggested. The committee is going to find things in the archive that raise questions about the unit's operational history beyond the Exchange relationship specifically."

"I know," she said. "I have worked in this unit for seven years. I have been the person asking questions about it for five of those years. The Exchange relationship was not the only thing I was asking about. It was the most significant thing I could document." She paused. "Whatever else they find — I want them to find it. The unit has been operating in ways that I understand better than anyone outside it and still do not fully understand. An external review is going to see things I missed."

"You're not concerned about what they'll find?"

She was quiet for a moment. "I am concerned that what they find will be used in ways that obscure what I was trying to do. That the documentation of my seven years of questions will be read as insufficient — as not enough asking, not enough escalation. That's a legitimate concern. But the alternative is that the investigation doesn't look fully, and the unit continues to operate in structures it doesn't understand, and that is worse." She paused. "I'll answer every question they ask. I'll provide everything I have. And then the investigation will find what it finds, and the record will say what it says."

It was the same answer she had given him in various forms since the first conversation, seven months ago. The same posture: complete cooperation, no illusion that cooperation resolved the moral complexity of the situation.

"The record will say what it says," he said.

"Yes."

He thanked her and closed the contact. He looked at his desk. Parren was gone. Derel was still here, cooperating formally with an investigation of a unit she had inherited and had tried to understand and had been prevented from understanding and had administered anyway. The record would include all of that — the inheritance, the questions, the obstruction, the administration.

He had said it to Maret about the settlement's second vote, and it was true of every element of this history: the harm and the after, the question and the block, the cooperation and the complication. The record would hold all of it. What the record did with all of it — that was the question the investigation was built to answer.

He picked up his pen and wrote the next liaison log entry.

chapters.title: Vol-012, Chapter 6: The Record, So Far
chapters.text:
The investigation's first formal working session was a Thursday. The committee held it in the oversight wing, not the chamber — a working room with a long table, natural light from the south wall's high windows, a documentation terminal at one end. Corin was not in the room. The session was not a designated session for the liaison position. He was three rooms away, at his own desk, working through the week's administrative correspondence.

At 1430, Iris came to his office.

She came without contacting him first, which was not her usual practice. She was precise about communication channels; she used the contact log for routine items and came in person when something was not routine. She sat across the desk from him and placed a printed document on the desk.

"The investigation's initiation entry," she said. "I want to confirm the framing before I file it."

He looked at the document. The registry entry was laid out in Iris's standard format — clean, economical, without editorializing. The header read: *Entry: Substrate Assessment Unit Investigation — Initiation. Date: [current].* The body of the entry described the motion, the vote, the investigation's authorized scope, the primary source documentation, and the committee's composition. At the bottom, under *Cross-references*, Iris had listed: the Exchange founding-client entry, the Eastern Field Calibration entry, the Parren censure entry, and the motion filing entries.

"The cross-references," Corin said.

"They tie the investigation to its documented origin points. The Eastern Field Calibration cross-reference may be contested — the investigation is formally about the institutional relationship, not the calibration specifically. But the relationship is part of what produced the conditions in which the calibration occurred. I want the cross-reference in."

"File it with the cross-reference."

She made a note. She did not move to leave.

"The registry count," she said. "Do you want to see it?"

He looked at her.

"I've been tracking it," she said. "The number of entries related to the Eastern Field Calibration and its institutional context. The full count, as of the investigation's initiation entry." She put a second printed sheet on the desk. "I don't usually generate these. But I wanted you to see it."

The sheet listed the entries in sequence: the initial calibration entry, the first formal acknowledgment, the Council motion, the Null Congregation reconciliation entries, the Exchange relationship entries, the Substrate Assessment Unit documentation entries, the Parren censure entries, the motion entries, and now the investigation initiation. The count at the bottom read: 34.

Thirty-four distinct registry entries, connected to the calibration and its institutional context. Twenty-three of them had been created in the past fourteen months — since the field expedition, since the monitoring arrays, since Cross began tracing the subsidiary contract chain.

"What's not in the count?" Corin asked.

"Entries from other registries — the Council's official deliberation record, the committee's formal files. I'm counting only what's in this registry. What I've been building." She was quiet for a moment. "The 216 are in three entries. One for the harm, one for the Council acknowledgment, one for the formal vote notation. Three entries for 216 people." She looked at him. "That's not sufficient. I know it's not sufficient. But the registry is not a person counter. It documents events and structures. I don't know how to make it do what's sufficient for those three entries."

Corin looked at the count sheet for a long time.

Thirty-four entries. The investigation was now open. The investigation had an entry. The entity's signal at the coordinate was at the highest level Lian had recorded — she had sent him the monitoring summary that morning, matter-of-fact, no interpretive language: *Signal at coordinate: highest recorded level. Pattern consistent with prior monitoring. No change in restoration rate.*

Fifteen percent. The restoration rate was still fifteen percent, ten months after the field expedition and the fixed monitoring arrays and all of it. The signal was stronger. The substrate was strengthening. The rate had not changed.

"How much of this is enough?" he asked.

It came out as a real question, not a rhetorical one. He had not intended to ask it aloud. But Iris was in the room and she was the person most likely to have thought about it.

She looked at the registry entry sheet on the desk.

"I don't know what enough looks like," she said. "I know what the record says so far."

The room was quiet.

Corin thought about what the record said so far. Thirty-four entries. The harm documented. The acknowledgment documented. The founding-client relationship documented. The community's second vote documented. The investigation documented.

The Archive was still the Archive. That was also true. The institution had survived the censure, and the motion, and the vote, and the investigation's opening. It had not ended. It had not transformed. It was still the institution that had run the Eastern Field Calibration, that had sustained the Exchange relationship for 44 years, that had built oversight mandates with gaps large enough to pass a commercial timeline-suppression partnership through without anyone noticing for four decades. It was also the institution that had, when the record became sufficient, voted unanimously to investigate itself.

The institution that had caused the harm and the institution voting to investigate itself. The record would have to hold both.

"The entity's signal," he said. "Lian sent the morning summary."

Iris looked at him.

"Highest recorded level," he said. "The pattern is the same. The restoration rate is the same." He paused. "Fifteen percent. Ten months."

"I saw the summary."

"The signal and the rate don't match. If the signal were increasing at that level, the restoration rate should be — something. Changing. Moving."

"Lian hasn't offered an interpretation of the gap."

"No." She had made one interpretive claim during the early field deployment record — that the entity's signal showed something consistent with deliberate action — and had been careful since about whether to make another. The caution was appropriate. The gap between the signal's strength and the rate's stagnation was visible in the monitoring data and had been visible for weeks. Lian had not offered an explanation, which meant she did not have one she was prepared to claim.

"The investigation is open," Corin said. "Parren is gone. The signal is the highest it's been. The rate is unchanged." He looked at the registry entry sheet. "I don't know what to do with that combination."

"You don't have to do anything with it," Iris said. "You have to document it."

He looked at her.

"You're the liaison," she said. It was not unkind. It was precise. "The combination of facts is not a problem you're required to solve. It's a situation you're required to track and report accurately."

"That's a limited role."

"It's the role you have. It's not a small role." She stood and collected the two printed sheets from his desk. "I'm going to file the investigation initiation entry. With the cross-references."

"With the cross-references," he said.

She went back to the registry hall. He could hear, faintly, the sound of the terminal as she worked.

---

That evening, before he left, he went to the monitoring hub.

Lian was still there, which he had not assumed she would be — it was past 1800 and the evening monitoring shift had taken over the primary arrays. But Lian was at her secondary station, the one she used when she wanted to work with the raw data rather than the processed summaries. She did not look up when he came in.

"The signal summary," he said.

"Yes." She did not turn around.

He waited.

She turned around. "You want to know about the gap."

"Between the signal level and the restoration rate."

She looked at him for a moment. She had the quality that very precise people sometimes had — a visible pause before speaking, while they determined whether what they were going to say was what they actually believed rather than what seemed expected. It was a pause he had come to recognize.

"The gap is consistent," she said. "Three months of data. The signal continues to rise. The rate does not change. I don't have a precedent for this in the monitoring archive. I have eighty years of data from before the extraction period and fourteen months of post-expedition data. The pre-extraction data doesn't show this kind of divergence between signal strength and substrate activity." She paused. "I have considered the possibility that I'm measuring the wrong thing — that the restoration rate metric is capturing something that doesn't correspond to what the signal is doing. But the restoration rate metric is the best proxy we have for actual substrate recovery in the unrestored territories. I don't have a better one."

"What would a better one require?"

"Field equipment. In the unrestored territories. What we have are the fixed arrays, which are in the perimeter — they detect the signal, but they're not measuring the substrate directly. The field expedition's equipment could measure the substrate directly at the coordinate. We have not sent another expedition." She looked at him. "The monitoring committee has not authorized one."

He thought about that. The field expedition had taken three months to authorize and had required a specific set of circumstances — the initial anomaly detection, the Congregation's partial opening, the coordination with the eastern communities. A second expedition was a different matter.

"The entity's signal," he said. "At the highest level you've recorded. Does it look like the signal from before anything was extracted? Before the extraction period?"

She was quiet again. The precise pause.

"Some elements of it match the pre-extraction pattern. The frequency architecture is similar in some harmonics. But there are elements that don't match the pre-extraction data." She turned back to her screen and brought up a waveform display — two signals overlaid, one in a darker line, one lighter. "The darker line is the pre-extraction baseline, from 80 years ago. The lighter line is what we're reading now." She pointed to a cluster of harmonics in the right third of the display. "These match. Within the margin of my arrays' resolution." She moved her finger to the left third. "These don't exist in the pre-extraction data. These are new."

Corin looked at the display.

"New as in not-yet-restored?" he asked. "Or new as in something-different?"

"I don't know." She said it without hesitation. "That is a question that requires field measurement to distinguish. The fixed arrays can tell me there's something that wasn't there before. They can't tell me what category of thing it is."

He looked at the waveform overlay. The investigation was open. Parren was gone. The record said what it said. The signal was doing something that the fixed arrays could see and could not explain.

"I'm going to note in the liaison log that the monitoring hub's current data includes uncharacterized elements in the coordinate signal, and that field measurement would be required to categorize them," he said.

Lian nodded. She had already turned back to her secondary station.

He walked back to his office in the quiet of the evening facility and wrote the monitoring note in the liaison log, and the investigation initiation note, and the term-conclusion note for Parren's file, and everything else that had happened in the fourteen days of the deliberation period and the final two weeks of Parren's term.

Then he wrote at the bottom of the monitoring note, not for the log — just for himself, just to see whether it could be said: *The record says what it says. The signal says something the record doesn't cover yet. The investigation is open and Parren is gone and the rate is fifteen percent and the entity is at the coordinate doing something the fixed arrays can't characterize. The institution is still the institution. The ground at the coordinate is doing something.*

He deleted it. None of it was for the log.

But it was accurate.

He closed his terminal, and locked his office, and went home through the east corridor, which was the same corridor Parren had used on her last day, and the corridor looked the same as it always did, and the Archive was still the Archive, and the investigation was open, and the signal was at its highest level, and the record said what the record said so far.

---

Three days later, Cross brought him a preliminary note from the investigation committee.

It was not a formal finding — formal findings required committee consensus and documentation review. It was a preliminary note, which was the committee's informal way of saying: we have seen something and we want the relevant parties to be aware of it before we formally document it.

The note was two paragraphs. The first paragraph described a category of the Substrate Assessment Unit's archived field monitoring data that Cross had not previously examined: the unit's pre-founding-era monitoring records. The unit had been established thirty-two years ago, but it had absorbed the monitoring functions of an earlier office that had operated for twelve years before the unit's founding. The earlier office's records were in the unit's archive under a separate series designation — Series A, rather than the numbered series that the post-founding records used.

The second paragraph: in Series A, the committee had found two separate entries referencing signal anomalies at the calibration site coordinates. The first was from forty-one years ago — three years before the unit's founding, nine years before the Exchange founding-client relationship began. The second was from forty-four years ago — seven years before the unit's founding.

Both entries were classified. Both entries used a different classification system from the current unit's codes — the pre-founding classification codes, which had been partially converted but not fully migrated when the unit was established. Both entries, under the pre-founding classification system, were marked as requiring follow-up. The follow-up designations had no corresponding follow-up records.

Corin read the note twice. Then he read it a third time.

Cross had not added interpretive language. It was a preliminary note: here is what was found, here is what it appears to be, here is what we need to verify. The verification would take time. The committee would need to reconstruct what the pre-founding classification system's follow-up designations meant, and whether the absence of follow-up records indicated that the follow-up had been conducted and not preserved, or that it had not been conducted at all.

But the entries existed. Forty-one years ago and forty-four years ago, someone in the Archive's monitoring infrastructure had flagged an anomaly at the calibration site and marked it for follow-up.

The unit had been founded thirty-two years ago. The Exchange founding-client relationship had begun forty-four years ago. One of the entries predated the relationship by three years. The other coincided with it, within the same year.

He did not call Cross. The preliminary note did not require an immediate response — it required him to read it and acknowledge it and wait for the formal finding.

He acknowledged receipt and set the note on his desk beside the closed registry count sheet from the day Iris had shown it to him.

Thirty-four entries. This would be more.

The signal was doing something the fixed arrays could not characterize. The investigation was finding things that predated the investigation's original scope by decades. The record was going to need to be larger than anyone had planned.

He looked at the note for a long time.

Then he picked up his pen and wrote in the margin, for his own reference only: *What did they see, forty-one years ago? What did they decide to do, forty-four years ago, in the same year the Exchange relationship started? What are those two coincidences?*

He drew a line through it. It was not a finding. It was a question.

The investigation would answer it, or it would not, and either way the answer would be in the record.

He filed the note in his working folder and went back to his desk and opened the day's administrative correspondence and did his work.
